Once a simulation is active, this module is also responsible for generating real-time sensor data from the deployed virtual nodes. 

Concurrently, the system's automated Network Security Monitoring Module is active during simulation runs. This module is designed to detect malicious activity. A unique aspect of the system is that cyber-attack scenarios can be triggered by the User, which then causes a Virtual Node (acting as an attacker) to generate malicious traffic. The security module's primary function is to identify these anomalies and automatically trigger security alerts for further analysis. 

Finally, all runtime data is consolidated and presented to the User via the Performance Visualization Dashboard module. This interface allows the User to view real-time network performance metrics alongside the generated security alerts, providing comprehensive insights into the behavior of the simulated IoT environment.

2. ### **Use Case Descriptions** {#use-case-descriptions}

1. Register Account

| Item | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Use Case ID** | UC-01 |
| **Use Case Name** | **Register Account** |
| **Actor** | User (Student / Researcher) |
| **Description** | The user creates a new account to gain access to the Makmal-IoTInfra testbed environment. |
| **Pre-condition** | The Web Server (Flask) and Database containers are running. |
| **Main Flow** | 1\. The user accesses the web application and clicks "Sign Up". 2\. The system displays the Registration form. 3\. User enters Username, Email, and Password. 4\. User clicks "Register". 5\. System checks availability of the username/email. 6\. The system hashes the password and saves the new user record. 7\. The system displays "Account Created" and redirects to Login. |
| **Alternative Flow** | **5a. User Already Exists:** 1\. The system finds the username or email is already taken. 2\. The system displays an error: "User already exists." 3\. Users are prompted to choose a different username. |
| **Post-condition** | A new user record is created in the database. |

   *Table 4.1: Use Case Description for Register Account*

2. Login Account (Authenticate)

| Item | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Use Case ID** | UC-02 |
| **Use Case Name** | **Login Account (Authentication)** |
| **Actor** | User |
| **Description** | The user provides credentials to access the secure management interface of the testbed. |
| **Pre-condition** | The user has a registered account (UC-04a). |
| **Main Flow** | 1\. The user accesses the web application URL. 2\. The system displays the Login page. 3\. User enters Username and Password. 4\. User clicks "Login". 5\. The system validates credentials against the database. 6\. The system redirects users to the Main Dashboard. |
| **Alternative Flow** | **5a. Invalid Credentials:** 1\. The system finds no match for username/password. 2\. System displays "Invalid Login" error. 3\. Users are prompted to retry. |
| **Post-condition** | The user is authenticated and granted session access to system features. |

   *Table 4.2: Use Case Description for Login Account*

3. Manage Virtual IoT Device

| Item | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Use Case ID** | UC-03 |
| **Use Case Name** | **Manage Virtual IoT Devices** |
| **Actor** | User |
| **Description** | The user adds, edits, or deletes virtual IoT nodes to configure the network topology before a simulation run. |
| **Pre-condition** | 1\. The user is logged in to the Makmal-IoTInfra dashboard. 2\. The simulation is currently **stopped**. |
| **Main Flow** | 1\. The user navigates to the "Device Manager" page. 2\. The system displays a list of existing devices. 3\. User clicks **"Create New Node"**. 4\. System prompts for Device ID, MQTT Topic, and Sensor Type. 5\. The user enters the details and clicks "Save". 6\. The system validates that the Device ID is unique. 7\. The system saves the configuration to the database. 8\. System updates the device list to show the new node. |
| **Alternative Flow** | **6a. Duplicate ID Error:** 1\. The system detects the Device ID already exists. 2\. The system displays an error message: "ID already in use." 3\. The user enters a new ID. |
| **Post-condition** | The new IoT node is stored in the database and ready to be instantiated in the next simulation run. |

   *Table 4.3: Use Case Description for Manage Virtual IoT Devices*

4. Start Simulation Run

| Item | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Use Case ID** | UC-04 |
| **Use Case Name** | **Start Simulation Run** |
| **Actor** | User |
| **Description** | The user initiates the simulation, causing the system to spin up the virtual nodes and begin generating MQTT traffic. |
| **Pre-condition** | 1\. At least one virtual device has been registered (UC-01). 2\. The MQTT Broker container is running. |
| **Main Flow** | 1\. The user clicks the **"Start Simulation"** button on the main dashboard. 2\. The system retrieves the device configurations from the database. 3\. The system initiates the Node-RED flows for each registered device. 4\. The system begins publishing sensor telemetry to the MQTT broker. 5\. System updates the dashboard status indicator to "Running". 6\. The system displays a notification: "Simulation Started Successfully." |
| **Alternative Flow** | **2a. No Devices Found:** 1\. The system finds zero devices in the database. 2\. System displays error: "Please configure devices before starting." 3\. The simulation remains in "Stopped" state. |
| **Post-condition** | Live data is flowing through the network, and the "View Live Dashboard" features become active. |

   *Table 4.4: Use Case Description for Start Simulation Run*

5. Trigger Simulated Cyber-Attack

| Item | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Use Case ID** | UC-05 |
| **Use Case Name** | **Trigger Simulated Cyber-Attack** |
| **Actor** | User |
| **Description** | The user selects and executes a specific cyber-attack (e.g., DoS, Port Scan) against the virtual network to test security responses. |
| **Pre-condition** | 1\. The simulation is running (UC-04). 2\. The target virtual nodes are active. |
| **Main Flow** | 1\. The user navigates to the "Attack Simulation" menu. 2\. The user selects an attack type (e.g., **DoS SYN Flood**). 3\. User defined parameters (Target IP, Duration, Packet Rate). 4\. The user clicks "Launch Attack". 5\. The system executes the attack script (e.g., using hping3) inside the container. 6\. The system displays an "Attack in Progress" status indicator. 7\. System logs the attack start time for analysis. |
| **Alternative Flow** | **5a. System Overload:** 1\. Host resource usage exceeds safety thresholds. 2\. The system aborts the attack and displays "Resource Limit Reached." |
| **Post-condition** | Malicious traffic is generated on the network, triggering the IDS (if rules match). |

   *Table 4.5: Use Case Description for Trigger Simulated Cyber-Attack*

6. Show Security Alert

| Item | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Use Case ID** | UC-06 |
| **Use Case Name** | **Show Security Alerts** |
| **Actor** | User (Viewer) |
| **Description** | The system notifies the user immediately when the underlying IDS (Snort) detects a malicious pattern in the network traffic. |
| **Pre-condition** | 1\. The simulation is running (UC-04). 2\. The Intrusion Detection System (Snort) is active. |
| **Main Flow** | 1\. The background IDS detects a signature match (e.g., DoS Syn Flood). 2\. The system logs the alert into the security database. 3.The system automatically displays a visual popup or red indicator on the active dashboard. 4\. The user views the alert details (Source IP, Attack Type, Timestamp). 5\. The user acknowledges or dismisses the alert. |
| **Alternative Flow** | N/A (Triggered automatically by system events). |
| **Post-condition** | The alert is permanently archived in the logs for post-experiment analysis. |

   *Table 4.6: Use Case Description for Show Security Alerts*

7. View Live Dashboard

| Item | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Use Case ID** | UC-07 |
| **Use Case Name** | **View Live Dashboard** |
| **Actor** | User |
| **Description** | The user monitors the real-time status of the network, including traffic throughput, active nodes, and system health. |
| **Pre-condition** | The simulation is running and data is being published to InfluxDB. |
| **Main Flow** | 1\. The user clicks the "Dashboard" tab. 2\. System (Grafana) queries the InfluxDB database for the latest metrics. 3\. System renders visualization panels (Line charts for traffic, Gauges for CPU). 4\. User views the **Network Traffic Performance**. 5\. The user views the list of **Active Nodes**. 6\. Dashboard auto-refreshes every 5 seconds. |
| **Alternative Flow** | **2a. Database Connection Fail:** 1\. Grafana cannot reach InfluxDB. 2\. Dashboard displays "No Data" or connection error. |
| **Post-condition** | The user is informed of the current network state and performance. |

   *Table 4.7: Use Case Description for View Live Dashboard*

8. Download Test Report (Export Logs)

| Item | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Use Case ID** | UC-08 |
| **Use Case Name** | **Download Test Reports (Export Logs)** |
| **Actor** | User (Researcher) |
| **Description** | The user exports historical sensor data, traffic logs, and security alert history for external analysis. |
| **Pre-condition** | A simulation or experiment has been completed and data exists in the database. |
| **Main Flow** | 1\. The user navigates to the "Reports & Logs" section. 2\. The user selects the data type (e.g., "IDS Alerts" or "Sensor Telemetry"). 3\. The user selects a date/time range. 4\. The user clicks **"Export to CSV"**. 5\. The system queries the database and compiles the file. 6\. The system triggers a browser download of the .csv file. |
| **Alternative Flow** | **5a. No Data:** 1\. Query returns zero results for the selected range. 2\. The system displays "No records found." |
| **Post-condition** | A CSV file containing the experimental data is saved to the user's local machine. |

   *Table 4.8: Use Case Description for Download Test Reports*

3. ### **Class Diagram** {#class-diagram}

   ![][image8]

   *Figure 4.2: Class Diagram*

Figure 4.2 presents the UML Class Diagram for the Makmal-IoTInfra system, providing a comprehensive blueprint of its static software structure. The diagram illustrates the system's modular architecture by defining key classes organized across distinct logical layers, including the User Interface, Data Entities, Simulation & Security, and Data Storage layers. It details the essential attributes and operational methods for core components, such as the SimulationManager, ContainerController, and SnortIDS, while clearly mapping the critical relationships and dependencies required to orchestrate the containerized testbed, handle data flow, and manage security events.

4. ### **Sequence Diagram** {#sequence-diagram}

A sequence diagram is a type of interaction diagram that shows how objects participate in a specific use case by exchanging messages in sequential order \[16\]. It visualizes the workflow and the dynamic behavior of the system. For the Makmal-IoTInfra project, sequence diagrams are used to illustrate the detailed flow of events for critical scenarios, including user access, simulation management, automated security monitoring, and data visualization. The following sections detail the sequence of interactions between actors, system components, and the database for these key use cases.

### **![][image9]**

*Figure 4.3: Register and Login Account*

Figure 4.3 illustrates the combined process for user registration and authentication, corresponding to Use Cases UC-01 and UC-02. The diagram utilizes an alternative (alt) frame to differentiate between two scenarios: a new user signing up and an existing user logging in. For a new user, the system presents a registration form, validates that the username does not already exist in the Database, and saves the new account credentials. For an existing user, the system accepts credentials, verifies them against the Database, and upon success, redirects the user to the main dashboard.

![][image10]

*Figure 4.4: Start Simulation Run*

Figure 4.4 depicts the process of initiating the IoT testbed simulation, as defined in Use Case UC-04. This sequence begins when an authenticated user triggers the "Start Simulation" action on the web interface. The System first communicates with the database to retrieve the configurations for all registered virtual devices. It then enters a loop, iterating through each device configuration and sending a command to the Container Controller to spin up the corresponding virtual node (container). Once all containers are successfully running, the system updates the user's view to reflect the active simulation status.

![][image11]

*Figure 4.5: Automated Intrusion Detection*

Figure 4.5 illustrates the automated background process of detecting security threats, triggering the alert generation aspect of Use Case UC-06. Unlike other scenarios, this process is triggered by system activity rather than direct user intervention. The sequence initiates when a Virtual Node (simulating an attacker) generates malicious traffic. This traffic is inspected by the Snort IDS. Upon detecting traffic that matches a known attack signature, Snort automatically generates an alert and sends the alert details to be permanently saved in the AlertLog database.

![][image12]

*Figure 4.6: View Live Dashboard*

Figure 4.6 shows how a user visualizes real-time data and security alerts, corresponding to Use Case UC-07. When the user navigates to the live dashboard (Grafana), the interface requests the latest data. The system then queries the necessary Databases for real-time metrics (from InfluxDB) and security log data (from SQLite). The retrieved data is then processed and rendered into charts, graphs, and alert popups for the user to review.

5. ### **Detail System Architecture** {#detail-system-architecture}

   ![][image13]

   *Figure 4.7: System Architecture Diagram*

The detailed system architecture of Makmal-IoTInfra is structured into interconnected layers. The User Layer provides the web-based interface for user interaction, connecting to the Application Layer where the Flask Web Server handles logic, authentication, and simulation management. This layer controls the Simulation Layer, where the Docker Platform runs isolated Node-RED containers as virtual IoT nodes. Facilitating data exchange is the Communication Layer, which uses an MQTT Broker to manage the flow of sensor telemetry and control messages between the virtual nodes and other system components.

The system is further supported by a Security Layer utilizing Snort IDS for real-time traffic monitoring and intrusion detection within the simulation network. Data is managed by the Data Layer, using InfluxDB for time-series metrics and SQLite for configuration storage. Finally, the Visualization Layer, powered by Grafana, consumes data from the storage and security layers to render live dashboards of network performance and security alerts for the user. This multi-layered approach ensures clear separation of concerns from simulation execution to data visualization.

## **4.2 System Design**  {#4.2-system-design}

This section details the detailed software design and data architecture of the Makmal-IoTInfra system. It defines the underlying database schemas, entity relationships, and data definitions required to support the simulation, management, and security monitoring modules described in the previous analysis.

### **4.2.1 Entity-Relational Diagram (ERD)** {#4.2.1-entity-relational-diagram-(erd)}

![][image14]

Figure 4.8: Entity-Relational Diagram (ERD) 

Figure 4.8 presents the Entity Relationship Diagram (ERD) for the Makmal-IoTInfra system. This diagram serves as the foundational blueprint for the system's backend database, visually representing how critical data entities are structured, stored, and related to one another. By defining these relationships, the ERD ensures data integrity and supports the complex interactions required between user management, simulation orchestration, and security monitoring.

The diagram is centered around four primary entities that govern the system's operation:

* **User:** This entity acts as the gatekeeper for system access, storing essential authentication credentials and profile information for individuals utilizing the testbed.  
* **Device:** This entity represents the "configuration blueprint" created by a user. It holds the static settings for a virtual IoT node, such as its intended sensor type and MQTT topic, before it is actively deployed.  
* **Container:** This entity embodies the "runtime instance" of a device. Once a simulation starts, a static Device configuration is instantiated as an active Docker Container, complete with runtime data like its assigned IP address and current operational state.  
* **SensorMetric:** This entity is responsible for the high-volume data collection within the testbed. It stores the actual time-series telemetry data generated by active containers during a simulation run, which is essential for real-time visualization and post-simulation analysis.  
* AlertLog: This entity serves as the system's security audit trail. It is responsible for permanently recording details of any malicious activity detected by the IDS during a simulation run, ensuring vital forensic data is preserved for analysis.

The relationships defined in Figure 4.10 illustrate the logical flow of data: a single **User** manages multiple **Device** configurations, which are deployed as active **Containers**. During runtime, these **containers** generate continuous telemetry stored as **SensorMetrics**. Simultaneously, the security layer monitors this traffic, generating **AlertLog** entries when threats are detected, providing a comprehensive data picture of the simulation session.

### **4.2.2 Data Dictionary** {#4.2.2-data-dictionary}

A. SQLite Tables (Configuration & Security) \[20\]

| Entity | Attribute | Type | Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **USER** | user\_id | **INT (PK)** | Unique ID for the admin/user. |
|  | username | VARCHAR | Login name. |
|  | password\_hash | VARCHAR | Securely hashed password (never store plain text). |

*Table 4.9: Data Dictionary User Entity*

This table 4.12 stores essential authentication information for system administrators and users. It includes a unique integer identifier for each user, their login username, and a securely hashed version of their password for authentication purposes.

| Entity | Attribute | Type | Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **DEVICE** | device\_id | **VARCHAR (PK)** | Unique string ID (e.g., "dev-001"). |
|  | user\_id | **INT (FK)** | Links to the User who owns this device. |
|  | mqtt\_topic | VARCHAR | The topic it publishes to (e.g., home/livingroom/temp). |
|  | status | ENUM | ENABLED or DISABLED (Config level). |

*Table 4.10: Data Dictionary Device Entity*

This table 4.13 contains the static configuration profiles for virtual IoT nodes. It records a unique string ID for each device, links it to the owner user, specifies the MQTT topic it will publish to, and holds its administrative status (enabled or disabled) prior to deployment.

| Entity | Attribute | Type | Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **CONTAINER** | container\_id | **VARCHAR (PK)** | The Docker Container ID (e.g., a1b2c3d4). |
|  | device\_id | **VARCHAR (FK)** | Links back to the Device config. |
|  | assigned\_ip | VARCHAR | The dynamic IP given by the Docker network. |
|  | status | ENUM | RUNNING, STOPPED, EXITED. |

*Table 4.11: Data Dictionary Container Entity*

This table 4.13 tracks the active runtime state of deployed virtual nodes. It maps a specific device configuration to a live Docker container by its ID, records the dynamically assigned IP address within the Docker network, and monitors its current operational status (such as running, stopped).

| Entity | Attribute | Type | Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **ALERT\_LOG** | alert\_id | **INT (PK)** | Auto-incrementing ID for each attack detected. |
|  | source\_ip | VARCHAR | The IP of the attacker (or compromised node). |
|  | attack\_type | VARCHAR | Snort rule name (e.g., "ICMP Flooding"). |
|  | severity | ENUM | LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH, CRITICAL. |

*Table 4.12: Data Dictionary Alert Log Entity*

This table 4.14 acts as a security audit trail, logging details of detected threats. For each security event, it records a unique auto-incrementing ID, the source IP address of the malicious traffic, the type of attack based on the Snort rule name, and the severity level of the threat.

B. InfluxDB (Time-Series Data)

| Entity | Key | Type | Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **SENSOR\_METRIC** | timestamp | **Time (PK)** | Exact nanosecond time of the reading. |
|  | device\_id | Tag (Indexed**)** | Allows fast filtering  |
|  | value | Field (Float) | The actual sensor number  |

*Table 4.13: Data Dictionary  Sensor Metric Entity*

This table 4.16 InfluxDB measurement is designed for high-speed storage of time-series telemetry data. It records the exact timestamp of each reading, uses the device ID as an indexed tag for efficient querying, and stores the numerical sensor value as a float field for analysis and visualization.

## **4.3 Detailed Module Description** {#4.3-detailed-module-description}

This section provides a detailed technical breakdown of the Makmal-IoTInfra system. It begins with a general description of the proposed solution. Next, it describes the four core modules defined in the introduction. Then, it shows process flowcharts for important system actions. Finally, it explains how the whole system works together based on the architecture diagram.

### **4.3.1 Description of Proposed System** {#4.3.1-description-of-proposed-system}

Makmal-IoTInfra is designed as a software-based testbed for IoT. Its main goal is to solve the problems of using physical labs by using Docker container technology to simulate IoT devices and networks. The system proposes a modular solution. It combines user management, simulation control, security checking, and data visualization into one platform that users access with a web browser. The system brings together standard industry tools. It uses Flask for management, Node-RED for simulation logic, Snort for security, and Grafana for visualization. This provides a complete environment for researching IoT scenarios and cyber threats without requiring specialized hardware.

### **4.3.2 Module Purpose and Functionality** {#4.3.2-module-purpose-and-functionality}

The system is divided into four main functional modules. The tables below detail the purpose, key components, primary functions, and interactions for each one.

| Feature | Details |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Purpose** | Acts as the central location for managing users and controlling the start and stop of simulations. |
| **Key Components** | Flask Web Framework, Docker SDK for Python, SQLite Database (User & Device tables). |
| **Primary Functions** | 1\. Provides web forms for users to register and log in. 2\. Allows creation of device settings (like sensor type and MQTT topic). 3\. Turns "Start/Stop" buttons in the UI into commands for the Docker engine using the SDK. |
| **Interactions** | Saves settings to SQLite. Sends start commands to the IoT Device Simulation Module. |

*Table 4.14: Module Description: Device Management & Control Module*

| Feature | Details |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Purpose** | The part responsible for running the virtual devices, creating sensor data, and handling communication. |
| **Key Components** | Docker Engine, Node-RED, Eclipse Mosquitto (MQTT Broker), InfluxDB. |
| **Primary Functions** | 1\. Automatically starts Node-RED containers based on saved settings. 2\. Creates fake sensor data or malicious network traffic. 3\. Handles the exchange of messages using the MQTT broker. 4\. Stores fast-moving sensor data into InfluxDB. |
| **Interactions** | Receives commands from Device Management. Sends network traffic that is checked by the Security Module. Sends data to the Visualization Module. |

*Table 4.15: Module Description: IoT Device Simulation Module*

| Feature | Details |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Purpose** | Provides automatic threat detection in the background within the simulated network. |
| **Key Components** | Snort IDS, Snort Ruleset, Log Parser Script, SQLite Database (AlertLog table). |
| **Primary Functions** | 1\. Look at network data packets on the Docker bridge network. 2\. Matches traffic against known attack patterns (like DoS or scanning). 3\. Reads the raw logs from Snort and stores organized alert data in SQLite. |
| **Interactions** | Monitors traffic from the Simulation Module. Provides alert data to the Visualization Module. |

*Table 4.16: Module Description: Network Security Monitoring Module*

| Feature | Details |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Purpose** | The part that shows real-time data about the simulation health and security status. |
| **Key Components** | Grafana, InfluxDB Data Source, SQLite Data Source. |
| **Primary Functions** | 1\. Asks for the latest sensor data from InfluxDB. 2\. Asks for past security alerts from SQLite. 3\. Creates interactive charts and tables shown in the main web UI. |
| **Interactions** | Reads data from both the Simulation and Security module databases. |

*Table 4.17: Module Description: Performance Visualization Dashboard Module*

### **4.3.3 Process Flow of Selected Modules** {#4.3.3-process-flow-of-selected-modules}

The flow charts below show the steps for two important system processes. They show the internal logic of starting a simulation and detecting a threat.

![][image15]

*Figure 4.9: Process Flow \- Starting a Simulation Run.*

 *This flow shows how the Device Management module starts the Simulation module.*

![][image16]

*Figure 4.10: Process Flow \- Automated Intrusion Detection System.* 

*This flow shows the interaction between attacker nodes, the network, and the Security module.*

### **4.3.4 System Operation (Architecture Walkthrough)** {#4.3.4-system-operation-(architecture-walkthrough)}

![][image17]

The way the system works matches the layers shown in Figure 4.5.8 (System Architecture Diagram).

The process starts at the User Layer. An authorized user uses the web interface in the Application Layer (Flask). The user creates device settings, which are saved in the Data Layer (SQLite). When the user starts a simulation, the Application Layer sends commands to the Simulation Layer. The Simulation Layer uses Docker to create virtual nodes. These nodes talk to each other using the MQTT broker in the Communication Layer. At the same time, normal sensor data flows into InfluxDB in the Data Layer. While the simulation runs, the Security Layer (Snort IDS) checks the traffic going through the Communication Layer. If it finds attack patterns, it creates alerts and saves them to SQLite in the Data Layer. Finally, the Visualization Layer (Grafana) gets the sensor data from InfluxDB and the alert logs from SQLite. It turns this data into a single dashboard for the user in the User Layer.

## **4.8 Technology** {#4.8-technology}

This section outlines the hardware and software technology requirements necessary for the development and operation of the Makmal-IoTInfra system. It details the specifications used during the development phase and lists the complete technology stack utilized in the implementation.

### **4.8.1 Hardware Requirements** {#4.8.1-hardware-requirements}

The development and execution of the system, particularly given its reliance on Docker containerization to run multiple services simultaneously (Node-RED, Snort, Databases), require adequate hardware resources. Table 4.8 lists the minimum used during development and recommended hardware specifications.

| Item | Minimum Requirement (Development) | Recommended Requirement |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Hardware** | Laptop / Desktop Computer | Laptop / Desktop Computer |
| **Operating System** | Windows 10/11 64-bit or Linux Ubuntu 20.04 LTS | Windows 10/11 64-bit or Linux Ubuntu 22.04 LTS |
| **Processor** | AMD Ryzen 5 5500U @ 2.10 GHz (or Intel Core i5 equivalent) | Intel Core i7 or AMD Ryzen 7 equivalent with virtualization support |
| **RAM** | 8.00 GB | 16 GB GB or higher |
| **Storage** | 256 GB SSD (with at least 20GB free space) | 512 GB SSD or higher |

*Table 4.18: Hardware Requirements*

### **4.8.2 Software Requirements** {#4.8.2-software-requirements}

The system is built using a specific set of software tools, frameworks, databases, and supporting applications. Table 4.9 below lists the software requirements used to develop and run the Makmal-IoTInfra system.

| Category | Requirement | Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Programming Language** | Python 3.8+ | Used for backend logic, the Flask web server, and automation scripts. |
| **Integrated Development Environment (IDE)** | Visual Studio Code | The primary code editor used for development. |
| **Containerization Platform** | Docker Desktop / Docker Engine | Essential for running the isolated simulation nodes and system services. |
| **Web Framework** | Flask \[13\] | A Python microframework used to build the web-based user interface. |
| **IoT Simulation Tool** | Node-RED \[10\]\[18\] | A flow-based tool running in containers to simulate IoT sensors and attack traffic. |
| **MQTT Broker** | Eclipse Mosquitto \[8\] | Handles communication between simulated devices. |
| **Security Tool** | Snort IDS \[11\] | Open-source network intrusion detection system for monitoring traffic. |
| **Database (Relational)** | SQLite \[20\] | Stores structured data like user accounts and device configurations. |
| **Database (Time-Series)** | InfluxDB \[12\] | Stores high-speed, real-time sensor telemetry data. |
| **Visualization Tool** | Grafana \[17\] | Provides the interactive dashboard for viewing data and alerts. |
| **Documentation** | Google Docs | Cloud-based word processing tool used for collaborative report writing and documentation. |
| **Supporting Tool** | diagrams.net (draw.io) | Used for designing system architecture diagrams, use cases, and process flowcharts. |

*Table 4.17: Software Requirements*

5. # **SDG ALIGNMENT** {#sdg-alignment}

This project aligns directly with **Sustainable Development Goal 4 (Quality Education)**, which aims to ensure inclusive and equitable quality education while promoting lifelong learning opportunities for all. The adoption of Internet of Things (IoT) technologies in education has been widely recognized as a key driver for improving teaching effectiveness and learner engagement by enabling interactive, data-driven, and context-aware learning environments \[21\], \[22\]. Prior studies show that IoT-supported educational platforms enhance digital literacy and practical technical skills by allowing learners to interact with real-world systems rather than relying solely on theoretical instruction \[23\]. By providing a low-cost, hands-on IoT infrastructure testbed, IoT-InfraLab supports SDG 4 by improving access to quality technical education, particularly in institutions with limited physical laboratory resources.

In addition, IoT technologies contribute to **inclusive and adaptive education**, which is a central pillar of SDG 4\. Smart learning environments powered by IoT enable real-time data collection and system monitoring, allowing educators to tailor learning activities to diverse learner needs and learning styles \[24\]. Research indicates that IoT-driven educational systems support personalized learning experiences and improve student participation by integrating physical and digital learning spaces \[25\]. Through its modular and software-based design, IoT-InfraLab enables flexible experimentation and remote access, helping to reduce educational barriers and promote equitable learning opportunities for students regardless of resource constraints.

Furthermore, the project emphasizes **experiential and collaborative learning**, which has been shown to significantly improve problem-solving, critical thinking, and teamwork skills in STEM education \[26\]. Studies on IoT-based learning frameworks demonstrate that hands-on experimentation with real networked systems prepares students for real-world engineering and cybersecurity challenges while fostering innovation and lifelong learning habits \[27\]. By enabling learners to design, monitor, and secure IoT networks in a controlled environment, IoT-InfraLab supports SDG 4 by equipping students with industry-relevant competencies and encouraging continuous learning beyond formal education settings.

6. # **CONCLUSION & FUTURE WORK** {#conclusion-&-future-work}

This project successfully presents the design and development of IoT-InfraLab, a web-based platform intended to support hands-on learning, experimentation, and analysis of IoT networks, network management, and cybersecurity. The system provides an integrated environment that allows users to simulate IoT devices, monitor network traffic, visualize system performance, and analyze security events through a centralized dashboard. By combining simulation, data management, and visualization components, the proposed solution addresses the limitations of traditional learning approaches that rely heavily on theoretical instruction and limited access to physical laboratory resources.

The results demonstrate that IoT-InfraLab can effectively support experiential learning by enabling users to explore real-world IoT scenarios in a controlled and low-cost environment. The platform enhances users’ understanding of IoT system architecture, network behavior, and basic security monitoring concepts, while also promoting technical skills such as system configuration, data analysis, and problem solving. Overall, the project meets its stated objectives and provides a solid foundation for further development in IoT education and security research.

For future work, several enhancements can be considered to further improve the functionality and realism of the platform. First, the integration of **real physical IoT hardware devices**, such as sensors and microcontrollers, would allow hybrid experimentation that combines simulated and real-world data. Second, the inclusion of an **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)** would strengthen the security layer by enabling active threat mitigation in addition to detection. Finally, incorporating **machine learning techniques** into the security system, particularly within an **Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS)**, could improve the accuracy and adaptability of threat detection by identifying complex and evolving attack patterns.
